coredata.dc:title: Coal tar pitch derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots with adjustable particle size for photocatalytic hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Jinpeng
Xiao, Nan
Wang, Yuwei
Li, Hongqiang
Liu, Chang
Xiao, Jian
Wei, Yibo
Guo, Zhen
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable size were synthesized form coal tar pitch through a simple hydrothermal method using NaOH as additive. The chemical composition and morphological feature of the obtained CDs were characterized. The prepared CDs were high crystallinity in nature. The size of CDs could be regulated by varying the addition amount of NaOH. By increasing the CDs’ size from 1.9 to 5.8 nm, the CDs supported Pt show a 70% improvement in the solar-to-hydrogen efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Adjustable particle size

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation process of CDs.
TEM images of CDs, (a) CDs-A 0.1, (b) CDs-A 0.15, (c) CDs-A 0.2, (d) CDs-A 0.25 (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of CDs (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
(a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs; (b) survey XPS spectrum, (c) high-resolution C 1 s spectrum, and (d) high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of CDs-A 0.1 (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
A proposed formation mechanism of CDs prepared from nitrified pitch.
(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs; (b) the corresponding Tauc plots (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
(a) Time courses of the production of H2 using CDs-A 0.25. (b) Time courses of H2 evolution of CDs in the rate of solar (AM 1.5G) (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
The relative atomic percentage of CDs.
The relative atomic percentage of N.

head.ce:title: Coal tar pitch derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots with adjustable particle size for photocatalytic hydrogen generation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals
Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering
PSU-DUT Joint Center for Energy Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with tunable size were synthesized form coal tar pitch through a simple hydrothermal method using NaOH as additive. The chemical composition and morphological feature of the obtained CDs were characterized. The prepared CDs were high crystallinity in nature. The size of CDs could be regulated by varying the addition amount of NaOH. By increasing the CDs’ size from 1.9 to 5.8 nm, the CDs supported Pt show a 70% improvement in the solar-to-hydrogen efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Efficient utilization of solar energy through photocatalytic water splitting has been deemed to be an environment-friendly and sustainable approach to address global warming and energy crisis [1,2]. The key issue of photocatalytic water splitting is to develop high-performance and low-cost semiconductor photocatalysts. Metal containing semiconductors are the state-of-the-art photocatalysts for hydrogen generation. However, they suffer from tedious preparation process, high cost, environmental incompatibility and poor stability [3]. Therefore, developing a low-cost, stable and high-performance sunlight-driven metal-free photocatalyst is still significant and challenging [4,5].

Generally speaking, an ideal metal-free photocatalyst is suggested to have high crystallinity and large specific surface area [6]. The former facilitates the transport of photogenerated excitons to the catalyst surface, while the later provides more active sites. Unfortunately, these two characteristics are often conflicting. Carbon dots (CDs), which are defined as surface-passivated graphene cores with the size less than 10 nm, can possess both high crystallinity and large specific surface area. Besides, their good chemical stability, biocompatibility [7] and low cost [8] make them a potential photocatalyst [9].

However, the solar to hydrogen efficiency of CDs is still far from meeting the industrial requirements resulting from the low efficiency of hole scavenging, weak light absorption and unsuited bandgap [6,10–13]. Some strategies have been developed to boost the CDs’ photocatalytic performance by regulating its crystalline structure and chemical composition. Reisner et al. indicated that a high crystallinity of carbon core can enhance the CDs’ specific activity in photocatalytic H2 evolution systems by improving their light absorption, while nitrogen-doping increases the efficiency of hole scavenging [13]. Teng et al. demonstrated that the doped nitrogen can repair the vacancy defects and facilitate electron transport, thereby promoting the CDs’ activity [10,11]. They also indicated that amino/amide group can promote the conjugation of the nitrogen lone-pair electrons with the aromatic π system, by which the CDs’ photocatalytic activity can be boosted [6]. In short, nitrogen doping and graphitized core of CDs are desirable for photocatalytic reactions.

Even more worrisome, the bandgap of CDs is too wide (>2.5 eV) to match with the major part of sunlight (450–700 nm, corresponding to 2.7–1.8 eV) [6]. To solve this issue, an effective and rational strategy to engineer the bandgap of CDs is a real need scientifically and technically. Theoretical calculations [12,14,15] have shown that the bandgap of CDs strongly depends on their sp2 domain size, which has been experimentally proven by Chen’s group [12]. By increasing the sp2 domain size, the bandgap of CDs reduced from 2.40 to 1.88 eV. In the case of CDs with graphitized core, the sp2 domain size can be approximately considered as their particle size. That is to say, the bandgap of CDs with graphitized core can be adjusted by tuning their particle size, by which the CDs’ photocatalytic activity can be improved. To this end, the synthesis of CDs with graphitized core and controllable particle size becomes the precondition. Up to now, CDs with controllable particle size have been synthesized successfully. However, the precursors used in these methods, e.g., citric acid, urea and glucose, are mostly nongraphitizable carbon precursors with high sp3 hybridized carbon content [13,16,17]. As a result, the cores of CDs are usually amorphous under low temperature condition (below 200 °C), which is disadvantageous to improve the catalytic performance of CDs. In the present paper, coal tar pitch with high sp2 hybridized carbon content is used as the raw material. Generally speaking, the coal tar pitch derived carbon materials are graphitizable. So it is an ideal precursor for synthesizing CDs with highly graphitized structure. So far, several strategies have been reported to synthesize coal and coal tar pitch derived CDs [18–20]. However, the synthesis of nitrogen doping and graphitized CDs with adjustable particle size is still a big challenge. Herein, we report a novel NaOH-assisted hydrothermal method to synthesize nitrogen-doping CDs with a controllable size and graphitized core from coal tar pitch under low temperature condition. NaOH plays a key role in regulating the size and edge functionalization of CDs. The relationship between the particle size of CDs and photocatalytic performance was studied preliminarily.

Coal tar pitch from Ansteel Group Corporation was used as precursor. NaOH, HNO3 and dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off = 3500 Da) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All reagents were used as received without any further purification.

The synthesis procedure of the CDs is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a typical procedure, 5 g coal tar pitch was nitrated in 200 mL HNO3 (14.4 M) at 60 °C under refluxing and stirring for 12 h. After that, the obtained suspension was filtrated with 0.45 μm membrane to remove the acid. And then, the resultant brown nitrified pitch (0.2 g) was dispersed in 50 mL NaOH solution with desired concentration (0.0075, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3 and 0.35 M) by ultrasonication for 4 h. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 20 h. After removing the precipitation through filtration, the filtrate was dialysed with cellulose ester dialysis bags to remove the sodium salt and unfused small molecules. The precipitation was denoted as P x, where x represents the concentration of NaOH. The CDs were finally obtained after freeze drying with a high yield of 18–23%. The final samples were denoted as CDs-A x, where x represents the concentration of NaOH. For comparison, ammonia solution (20% in water) was used to replace NaOH to synthesize CDs. However, no CDs formed in the ammonia solution.

The morphologies and structures of the samples were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips Tecnai G220) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM, TF30). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Rigaku D/Max-2400 diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were scanned by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10. The UV–vis absorption was recorded on a Thermofisher UV–vis spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by a Thermo VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with the monochromatized Al Kα as excitation source. Raman spectra were obtained by a Thermo Fisher Scientific DXR Raman microscope, using a 532 nm laser as the light source. The pH value of solution was measured by INSEA PHS-3C. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out on Zetasizer Nano-ZS 90.

Photocatalytic reactions were conducted in a gas-enclosed side irradiation system with an AM 1.5 G solar simulator (300 W Xe-lamp, PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd, China). In a typical process, 100 mg of the CDs were dispersed in a 90 mL 10 vol% TEOA aqueous solution by ultrasonic dispersion. To detect the gas production, a gas chromatograph (Agilent GC6890 N) with a 5 Å molecular sieves column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) was utilized. Nitrogen was used as the carrier gas with the flow rate of 40 mL min−1.

TEM images shown in Fig. 2 a–d illustrate that CDs are well dispersed without apparent aggregation. The particle size distribution statistics result (Fig. S1) shows that the concentration of NaOH can significantly influence the particle size of the CDs, i.e., by increasing the concentration of NaOH from 0.1 to 0.25 M, the average sizes of the CDs increase from 1.9 to 2.8, 4.3 and 5.8 nm. DLS measurements (Fig. S2) were carried out to further investigate the size of CDs. Despite the samples’ size distribution reflected by DLS are somewhat larger than those observed in TEM images, their variation trends are the same. By further increasing the concentration of NaOH to 0.3 and 0.35 M, the micron-sized carbon particles are obtained as shown in Fig. S3, which will lose their unique quantum characters. This may be due to the excessive intermolecular condensation at high alkali concentration. The XRD pattern reveals the presence of two peaks around 26° and 42°, which are corresponded with the (002) and (100) planes of carbon, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The small interlayer distance of 0.339 nm indicates the high crystallinity of the nano-sized graphene core. The interlayer lattice distance of CDs is 0.21 nm with respect to the (100) plane. Raman measurements were conducted to further investigate the effect of the NaOH concentration on the microstructure of CDs. The evolution of Raman spectra of the CDs obtained as a function of the NaOH concentration are given in Fig. 3b, in which two peaks are exhibited at around 1580 cm−1 (G band) and 1380 cm−1 (D band). The G band can be attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon, reflecting the formation of graphitic carbon, while the D band ascribes to the defects and imperfections. The Raman shifts can be used as an indicator of the average size of sp2 domain [21]. By increasing the NaOH concentration from 0.1 to 0.25 M, the G band red shifts from 1585 to 1578 cm−1 indicative of the increase of the sp2 domain size. The peak intensity ratio (ID/IG) progressively decreases from 1.03 to 0.85 by increasing the NaOH concentration due to the increased sp2 domain size, which are consistent with the Raman shifts. A well-resolved crystal lattice with spacing of 0.21 nm in the HRTEM image (Fig. S4) fits well with the carbon’s (100) facet indicating the CDs’ high crystallinity, which is favorable for enhancing its light absorption.

On the basis of the above observations, we suppose that the size of carbon dots can be regulated by changing the concentration of alkaline solution. After the reaction, the pH values of all the reacted solution were measured. It is interesting that all the pH values decreased to about 12.15 (11.73–12.45) at the end of the reaction, suggesting there is a threshold for this reaction. To further verify this result, A 0.0075 with initial pH value of 11.88 was prepared under the same condition. After hydrothermal reaction, the solution was filtrated and dialysed, it is interesting to note that the filtrate becomes colorless and transparent after dialysis (Fig. S5), indicating that no CDs existed in the filtrate. This result verifies that CDs cannot be formed when the pH of the alkali solution was lowered below 12.15. In short, the pH value plays a key role in the formation and size control of CDs in the present experiment condition, i.e., the particle sizes gradually increase by increasing the NaOH concentration in the pH range from 12.15 to 13.40.

In order to further understand the mechanism of the reaction, we tested the nitrified pitch, CDs-A 0.1, P 0.0075 and P 0.1 by the FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. S6, a rather broad adsorption band centered at 3450 cm−1 is assigned to the O–H vibration. Compared with the nitrified pitch, the O–H vibration of CDs-A 0.1 becomes stronger, which can be ascribed to the edge functionalization (-OH) of the CDs during the hydrothermal reaction. In the FT-IR spectrum of nitrified pitch, two strong vibrations at 1330 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1 can be assigned to the presence of the R–NO2, which disappears in that of CDs-A 0.1. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the samples with different particle size show the similar FT-IR spectra indicating that the present method can regulate the CDs’ particle size without significantly changing their surface chemical composition. Interestingly, the FT-IR spectrum of P 0.0075 is quite similar to that of nitrified pitch, as shown in Fig. S7, further confirming that the reaction cannot be carried out in a low concentration NaOH solution.

The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows strong signals of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s as well as weak signals of adsorbed Na+ on the surface of CDs [15]. To avoid the suspected influence of Na+ on the absorption and photocatalysis, post-treatments by diluted hydrochloric acid were carried out to remove the residual Na+ (Fig. S8). Atomic percentages of the C, N, and O elements in CDs-A 0.1 are calculated to be 74.72%, 5.23% and 20.05%, respectively. Fig. 4c illustrates the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of CDs-A 0.1, which can be fitted into sp2 C (CC/C–C) at 284.6 eV, sp3 C (C–C) at 285.3 eV, C–OH/C–N at 286.3 eV, CO at 287.6 eV, and O–CO at 289 eV [22]. Fig. S9 shows the high-resolution C 1s spectra of all the four CDs and the relative percentage of C derived from the XPS spectra are shown in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, the relative atomic percentage of sp2 C increases with the increase of the NaOH concentration, indicating that CDs-A 0.25 has the largest sp2 carbon domain. This corresponds to the results obtained from the Raman spectrum.

The N 1s high resolution spectrum of CDs-A 0.1 is shown in Fig. 4d, which can be converted into pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.6 eV), graphitic N (400.7 eV), and nitro N (405.4 eV) [23,24]. Fig. S10 shows the high resolution N 1s spectra of all the four CDs and the relative percentage of N derived from the XPS spectra are shown in Table S1. The content and species of nitrogen of all the CDs’ samples are quite similar. However, the nitro group content of the nitrified pitch is much higher than that of CDs-A 0.1 (Table 2 ), implying the formation of CDs is accompanied by consuming the nitro group [25]. This corresponds to the results obtained from the FT-IR. As shown in Fig. S11, there is almost no pyridine nitrogen and 10.92% pyrrole nitrogen in nitrified pitch. However, the contents of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen of CDs-A 0.1 increase to 9.37% and 56.68% respectively, suggesting that in the process of hydrothermal synthesis, two nitrified pitch molecules are bonded by forming new C–N–C bond.

On the basis of the above observations, a possible synthesis mechanism is proposed, as shown in Fig. 5 . We speculate that the CDs formed through “bottom-up” syntheses, in which process the dissolved nitrified pitch converts into CDs with the action of NaOH. Therefore, the reaction can continuously carry out, only when the nitrified pitch is dissolved in NaOH solutions. It has been reported that, nitrified pitch is one kind of amphiphilic carbonaceous material, which can be dissolved in alkaline aqueous solutions [26]. The threshold for this reaction may be the minimum pH value for nitrified pitch dissolving. The dissolved nitrified pitch will be functionalized by NaOH and convert part of nitro to N (transition state). The N is bonded with the aromatic ring to form C–N–C, by which, the CDs formed [25]. In the above reactions, NaOH is consumed. When the pH values are lower than 12.15, the hydrothermal reaction terminated. In this process, NaOH play a crucial role in dissolving nitrified pitch and edge functionalization [25], which is quite important for the occurrence of the reaction. On the other hand, the higher pH value of the initial solution, the more NaOH consumes, resulting in an increased extent of reaction and lager size of CDs. And, when the concentration of NaOH is larger than 0.3 M, the micron-sized carbon particles are formed.

The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out to reveal how the particle size affects the CDs’ light adsorbing ability. As shown in Fig. 6 a, by increasing the CDs’ particle sizes, their light adsorbing abilities are improved, and CDs-A 0.25 exhibits the strongest light absorption capacity. The bandgap energies of the CDs were determined by plotting the square of the absorption energy (αE2, where α is the absorbance and E is the photon energy) against E. The linear extrapolation in Fig. 6b reveals that the bandgap of CDs-A 0.1, CDs-A 0.15, CDs-A 0.2, and CDs-A 0.25 are 3.17, 3.13, 3.07 and 2.94 eV, respectively, which decrease with the increase of the CDs’ size. This result is consistent with the previous reports, i.e., the carbons’ bandgap is inversely proportional to their effective conjugation length or sp2 domain size [12,14,15]. Increasing the CDs’ size can enlarge their sp2 carbon domain, as indicated by Table S1, by which the bandgap of CDs has been narrowed. As expected, CDs-A 0.25 with the maximum size possesses the narrowest bandgap and the strongest light adsorbing ability among all. The valence band XPS was used to obtain the information of valence band (VB). The VB of CDs was found to be at 2.12, 2.07, 2.14 and 2.13 eV (Fig. S12). Therefore, the conduction band (CB) potential of CDs are calculated to be −1.05, −1.06, −0.93 and −0.81 eV from the formula ECB = EVB- Eg (Eg, ECB and EVB indicate the band gap energy, CB potential and VB potential), and the band structure diagram is illustrated in Fig. S13.

Fig. 7 a presents the time course involved in the production of H2 from the TEOA solution over a period of 100 h. H2 steadily evolved in the system without decay in activity demonstrating the high stability of CDs. As shown in Fig. S14, the photocatalytic hydrogen generation performance of CDs without Pt is quiet low, but the trend is obvious, i.e. the CDs’ photocatalytic performances are enhanced by increasing their size. For a clearer elucidation of how the particle size affect the CDs’ photocatalytic performance, 3 wt% Pt was deposited as an electron sink (or a cocatalyst), which substantially increases the photocatalytic activity of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 7b, the CDs-A 0.25 with the narrowest bandgap (thus widest absorption range and strongest light absorption ability) gives the highest H2 yield (60.75 μmolH2 g−1 h−1), which is about 1.7 times higher than that of the CDs-A 0.1.

In general, CDs with adjustable particle size were synthesized from coal tar pitch through NaOH-assisted hydrothermal carbonization. We propose a formation mechanism of CDs including edge functionalization, formation of carbon nitrogen bond, dehydration and carbonization. By increasing the particle size, the CDs show a decreased bandgap and boosted light adsorbing ability, which can be described to the enlarged sp2 domain. As a result, 70% improvement has been realized in H2 evolution (60.75 μmolH2 g−1 h−1) by increasing the size of CDs supported Pt from 1.9 nm to 5.8 nm.

Jinpeng Bai: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Nan Xiao: Supervision, Conceptualization. Yuwei Wang: Software. Hongqiang Li: Formal analysis. Chang Liu: Investigation. Jian Xiao: Resources. Yibo Wei: Resources. Zhen Guo: Software. Jieshan Qiu: Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Nos. U1508201 , U1710117 and U2003216), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. DUT20LAB131).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.10.088.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dramatic activity of C3N4/BiPO4 photocatalyst with core/shell structure formed by self-assembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Pan, J. Xu, Y.J. Wang, D. Li, Y.F. Zhu. Dramatic Activity of C3N4/BiPO4 Photocatalyst with Core/Shell Structure Formed by Self-Assembly. Adv. Funct. Mater. 2012;22(7):1518-1524.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spatially separated CdS shells exposed with reduction surfaces for enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Defects induced efficient overall water splitting on A carbon-based metal-free photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhu, M.M. Zhu, Y. Sun, Y.J. Zhou, H. Huang, Y. Lifshitz, et al. Defects Induced Efficient Overall Water Splitting on A Carbon-Based Metal-Free Photocatalyst. Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 2018;237:166-174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Borocarbonitrides as metal-free catalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.N.R. Rao, M. Chhetri. Borocarbonitrides as metal-free catalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction. Adv. Mater. 2019;31(13):1803668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible- and NIR-light responsive black-phosphorus-based nanostructures in solar fuel production and environmental remediation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Feng, W.Y. Lei, G. Liu, M.H. Liu. Visible- and NIR-light responsive black-phosphorus-based nanostructures in solar fuel production and environmental remediation. Adv. Mater. 2018;30(49):1804770.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Architecting nitrogen functionalities on graphene oxide photocatalysts for boosting hydrogen production in water decomposition process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L-C. Chen, C-Y. Teng, C-Y. Lin, H-Y. Chang, S-J. Chen, H. Teng. Architecting Nitrogen Functionalities on Graphene Oxide Photocatalysts for Boosting Hydrogen Production in Water Decomposition Process. Adv. Energy Mater. 2016;6(22):1600719.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S Perumal, N Muthuchamy, Y.R. Lee. Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications. Fuel. 2020;275:117821.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, K.R. Aseer, S Perumal, N Karthik, Y.R. Lee. Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink. Biosens. Bioelectron. 2018;99:303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon and graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic and energy devices: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.M. Li, M.C. Rui, J.Z. Song, Z.H. Shen, H.B. Zeng. Carbon and Graphene Quantum Dots for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices: A Review. Adv. Funct. Mater. 2015;25(31):4929-4947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots as photocatalysts for overall water-splitting under visible light illumination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T-F. Yeh, C-Y. Teng, S-J. Chen, H. Teng. Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots as photocatalysts for overall water-splitting under visible light illumination. Adv. Mater. 2014;26(20):3297-3303.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic effect of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities for graphene-based quantum dots used in photocatalytic H2 production from water decomposition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T-F. Yeh, S-J. Chen, H. Teng. Synergistic Effect of Oxygen and Nitrogen Functionalities for Graphene-Based Quantum Dots Used in Photocatalytic H2 Production from Water Decomposition. Nano Energy 2015;12:476-485.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Systematic bandgap engineering of graphene quantum dots and applications for photocatalytic water splitting and CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.B. Yan, J. Chen, N. Li, J.Q. Tian, K.X. Li, J.Z. Jiang, et al. Systematic bandgap engineering of graphene quantum dots and applications for photocatalytic water splitting and CO2 reduction. ACS Nano 2018;12(4):3523-3532.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing light absorption and charge transfer efficiency in carbon dots through graphitization and core nitrogen doping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.C.M. Martindale, G.A.M. Hutton, C.A. Caputo, S. Prantl, R. Godin, J.R. Durrant, et al. Enhancing Light Absorption and Charge Transfer Efficiency in Carbon Dots through Graphitization and Core Nitrogen Doping. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2017;56(23):6459-6463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen. Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications. Small 2015;11(14):1620-1636.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Qu, D. Zhou, D. Li, W.Y. Ji, P.T. Jing, D. Han, et al. Toward efficient orange emissive carbon nanodots through conjugated sp2-domain controlling and surface charges engineering. Adv. Mater. 2016;28(18):3516-3521.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cayuela, M.L. Soriano, C. Carrillo-Carrion, M. Valcarcel. Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency. Chem. Commun. 2016;52(7):1311-1326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and purification-free synthesis of nitrogenated amphiphilic graphitic carbon dots
